did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper titled 'Artificial intelligence algorithms for the automated detection and depth measurement of basal cell carcinoma in RCM-OCT images' explicitly used two artificial intelligence models trained via AIVIA's deep learning model to detect the stratum corneum and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in OCT rasters. The AI models were applied to a testing set to detect BCC and measure tumor depth, with results correlated to H&E images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper titled 'Artificial intelligence algorithms for the automated detection and depth measurement of basal cell carcinoma in RCM-OCT images: a pilot, proof-of-concept study' (Chunk 3) describes original experimental work. It details the training of two AI models using AIVIA's deep learning model on a specific dataset of OCT rasters (Chunk 3-4), the application of these models to a testing set, and the statistical correlation of results with H&E images (Chunk 4). The study produces new results regarding AI detection accuracy and depth measurement, fitting the criteria for a research paper rather than a review.

title: Artificial intelligence algorithms for the automated detection and depth measurement of basal cell carcinoma in RCM-OCT images: a pilot, proof-of-concept study
brief_summary: This pilot study developed two artificial intelligence models using AIVIA's deep learning software to automate the detection of the stratum corneum and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in reflectance confocal microscopy-optical coherence tomography (RCM-OCT) images. The models were trained on a dataset of 20 biopsy-confirmed BCC OCT rasters and applied to a separate testing set of 15 rasters. The main evaluation approach involved calculating tumor depth from AI predictions and correlating these measurements with histopathology (H&E) images using Pearson correlation. The key result showed a statistically significant correlation (r²=0.59, p=0.02) between AI-derived depth measurements and H&E measurements. The study concludes that AI may potentially aid in the non-invasive diagnosis and management of BCC, though future work is needed to expand training data and compare AI with human detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused on biopsy-confirmed basal cell carcinoma (BCC). The training set included 5 nodular BCC (nBCC), 5 superficial BCC (sBCC), and 10 mixed superficial and nodular BCC (s,nBCC). The testing set contained 1 nBCC, 1 sBCC, and 13 s,nBCC. The AI models were trained to detect BCC and the stratum corneum.

all_task_types.target: Stratum corneum (SC) and Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in OCT rasters
all_task_types.details: Two AI models were trained to detect the stratum corneum and BCC from OCT rasters using AIVIA's deep learning model. The models were applied to a testing set with a 95% confidence threshold to detect these structures.

Tumor depth was calculated from AI predictions and correlated to corresponding H&E images using the Pearson test (r²=0.59, p=0.02). While the output is a continuous measurement, the primary AI task described is detection, with depth calculation being a subsequent post-processing step for evaluation.

datasets_used.name: Biopsy-confirmed BCC OCT raster dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of OCT rasters from biopsy-confirmed basal cell carcinoma (BCC) cases, annotated using AIVIA imaging analysis software. The study is a pilot, proof-of-concept study conducted by researchers at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center and Federal University of Rio de Janeiro.

use.training: Used to train two AI models (detecting SC and BCC) via AIVIA's deep learning model using annotated images.
use.testing: Used to apply the trained AI models with a 95% confidence threshold to detect SC and BCC. Tumor depth from AI predictions was correlated with H&E images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To correlate tumor depth measurements derived from AI predictions with corresponding H&E images.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no linear correlation between tumor depth measurements derived from AI predictions and those from histopathology (H&E) images.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant linear correlation between tumor depth measurements derived from AI predictions and those from histopathology (H&E) images.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported a statistically significant correlation (r²=0.59, p=0.02) between AI-derived tumor depth measurements and H&E measurements, indicating that the correlation is unlikely to be due to chance at the 0.05 significance level.

AI_depth_measurement.comparison: Correlation between tumor depth measurements from AI predictions and corresponding H&E images

biopsy-confirmed_BCC_OCT_raster_dataset.notes: The training set included 20 OCT rasters (5 nBCC, 5 sBCC, 10 s,nBCC). The testing set contained 15 OCT rasters (1 nBCC, 1 sBCC, 13 s,nBCC). The AI models were trained using AIVIA's deep learning model on annotations made with AIVIA imaging analysis software.

architecture_details.details: Two separate AI models were trained: one for detecting the stratum corneum (SC) and one for detecting basal cell carcinoma (BCC).

training_details.dataset_composition: Training set included 5 nodular BCC (nBCC), 5 superficial BCC (sBCC), and 10 mixed superficial and nodular BCC (s,nBCC) OCT rasters.

conclusions: The two artificial intelligence models successfully detected the stratum corneum and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in all 15 OCT rasters from the testing set.
There was a statistically significant correlation (r²=0.59, p=0.02) between tumor depth measurements derived from AI predictions and those from corresponding histopathology (H&E) images.
AI may potentially aid in the non-invasive diagnosis and management of BCC.
Future work is needed to increase the training set size and compare human versus AI detection of BCC from OCT rasters.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot, proof-of-concept with a very small sample size (20 training, 15 testing OCT rasters), limiting statistical power and generalizability.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance in the testing set, with 13 out of 15 cases being mixed superficial and nodular BCC (s,nBCC), while nodular (nBCC) and superficial (sBCC) types are underrepresented (1 case each).

The AI models achieved 100% detection rates for both stratum corneum and BCC in the testing set, but this metric may be inflated due to the small sample size and lack of negative controls (non-BCC images).

The correlation between AI-derived tumor depth and histopathology (H&E) was moderate (r²=0.59, p=0.02), indicating that while statistically significant, there is substantial unexplained variance in depth measurement accuracy.

The study lacks external validation; data was derived from a single institution (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center and Federal University of Rio de Janeiro), raising concerns about population or device bias.

The paper explicitly notes that future work is needed to increase the training set size and compare human versus AI detection, acknowledging current limitations in robustness and comparative efficacy.
No information is provided regarding data leakage risks, cross-validation strategies, or independent test sets beyond the described split, which is a concern for small datasets.
